body_txt: A character quotes a seemingly made-up word that no one has ever heard. This is usually a word the writer just made up but is occasionally a real obscure, archaic or obsolete word; for instance, 400 years before we had computers, we had email , which is a raised or embossed image pressed into metal (cognate to "enamel," and pronounced EM-eye, because it's French), and "unfriend," which meant precisely what it does today (just at the tavern, rather than on Facebook ). A type of Neologism (for the many cases where the word is new), of which Scrabble Babble is a Sub-Trope . Some examples are another form of Malaproper . See also Delusions of Eloquence and Informed Obscenity . Characters who primarily talk in these are known as a Neologizer . This is not to be confused with Buffy Speak ; while in both cases, whatever the speaker is attempting to say generally still makes sense in context, Buffy Speak lacks nuance due to being rambling by nature (since they're trying to remember the actual word for something but failing), and at best results in new compound words, not entirely new words. For words that are actually not new, it may be a case of either Accidentally Correct Writing or Shown Their Work . If these made-up words are only used and are repeated in a pattern of some sort in a conversation, that's a Conlang . Named for an exchange from an episode of The Simpsons . Writer David X. Cohen invented the word "cromulent" when the showrunners Bill Oakley and Josh Weinstein challenged the team to think up several real-sounding, but fake, words. "Cromulent" is intended to mean "fine" or "acceptable"; the word "cromulent" has subsequently been used in real life by writers and politicians. The made-up word embiggen was later used in a completely cromulent paper on string theory. It's on pages 28 and 31 here. It has also been added to Webster's Dictionary and Dictionary.com.
